title: PoE Budget & Switch Margin Guide
summary: Understand switch PoE budget, safety margin, PoE standard assumption, powered-port count, endpoint mix, and per-device watts.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates total PoE load from a device mix and compares it with the switch power budget after reserving safety margin.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it before selecting or loading a PoE switch, adding cameras or access points, or checking whether IR, heaters, phones, and miscellaneous devices leave enough margin.
bullets: Early network design planning.
Expansion, migration, or upgrade review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining network tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Switch PoE budget (W)
items.description: The total PoE power budget available from the switch or injector.
items.whyItMatters: This is the total power pool shared by all powered endpoints. It is different from the switch input power or power-supply label.
items.commonMistake: Using the wrong datasheet number or assuming every port can deliver maximum power at once.

links.label: Translate watts into power planning
links.note: Use this when PoE load also affects UPS, circuit, or power planning.

items.label: Safety margin (%)
items.description: A reserve percentage kept unused to absorb power spikes, cable loss, cold starts, IR lighting, heaters, and real-world variance.
items.whyItMatters: PoE devices can draw more during startup, night mode, or feature activation than they do in a steady state.
items.commonMistake: Designing to exactly 100% of the PoE budget.

items.label: PoE standard (assumption)
items.description: The planning standard used as a reference for the powered-device environment.
items.whyItMatters: The standard helps frame expected power class behavior, but the tool still relies on entered wattage for actual load math.
items.commonMistake: Assuming the selected standard guarantees every port and device will behave the same way.

items.label: PoE ports used (count)
items.description: The number of ports expected to actively power devices.
items.whyItMatters: Port count is a sanity check against the endpoint mix and can reveal designs that fit by watts but not by available ports.
items.commonMistake: Checking total watts but forgetting physical powered-port count.

items.label: Cameras (count)
items.description: The number of PoE cameras powered by the switch.
items.whyItMatters: Camera count is often the largest predictable PoE load in security networks.
items.commonMistake: Counting cameras but not accounting for night-mode features.

items.label: Camera watts (each)
items.description: The expected wattage for each camera.
items.whyItMatters: Camera watts determine whether the camera group is a light load or the dominant PoE consumer.
items.commonMistake: Using daytime steady draw when IR, heaters, or PTZ motors increase demand.

items.label: Wireless APs (count)
items.description: The number of PoE wireless access points powered by the switch.
items.whyItMatters: APs may draw higher power when radios, multigig uplinks, or advanced features are active.
items.commonMistake: Using an older AP wattage assumption for newer higher-power access points.

items.label: AP watts (each)
items.description: The expected wattage for each wireless access point.
items.whyItMatters: This determines how much of the PoE pool is consumed by wireless before cameras or phones are considered.
items.commonMistake: Assuming all APs are low-power even when feature mode changes draw.

items.label: VoIP phones (count)
items.description: The number of PoE phones powered by the switch.
items.whyItMatters: Phone counts can add a steady baseline load across many ports.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring phones because each one is small individually.

items.label: Phone watts (each)
items.description: The expected wattage for each VoIP phone.
items.whyItMatters: Small per-device differences add up when many phones share the same switch budget.
items.commonMistake: Using one generic phone draw for mixed models.

items.label: Other devices (count)
items.description: The number of additional PoE devices not covered by cameras, APs, or phones.
items.whyItMatters: Other devices protect the estimate from undercounting miscellaneous powered endpoints.
items.commonMistake: Leaving other devices at zero because they are not part of the main design package.

items.label: Other watts (each)
items.description: The expected wattage for each additional PoE device.
items.whyItMatters: This gives miscellaneous endpoints a realistic power impact instead of hiding them in the margin.
items.commonMistake: Treating miscellaneous devices as free load.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the powered-device mix fits with practical margin. Watch means the design may work but deserves a closer look at startup draw and endpoint assumptions. Risk means the switch PoE budget may be overloaded.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the topology, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning mistakes come from checking one layer or one average value while ignoring peaks, overhead, path behavior, failure conditions, and growth.
bullets: Planning from theoretical line rate instead of usable sustained throughput.
Sizing for calm-period average traffic while ignoring peaks, bursts, retransmits, and maintenance activity.
Treating one network layer in isolation even though access, aggregation, WAN, VPN, and endpoint behavior interact.
Forgetting to document assumptions before the design becomes hardware, cabling, or service-provider work.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, firewall/router/switch limits, service-provider terms, cabling validation, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real measurements from the installed network are better than estimates whenever they are available.
Protocol overhead, encapsulation, device behavior, cabling, switching, routing, and WAN conditions can change the result.
Healthy results still need validation against the actual switch, firewall, router, ISP, tunnel, or endpoint platform being used.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, vendor validation, production testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, firmware, service-provider limits, and network architecture being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for performance, outage behavior, security, and operational recovery.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or topology changes.
